quotes.verbatim_quote:
The old style of strumming chords
for guitar will never do in modern playing, so we will have to work out a complete system. With this style of
chords you will learn to play chord solos, make all kinds of introductions, play background for horn players, and all
kinds of tricky fill-ins.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the book's central premise: rhythm strumming is obsolete and must be replaced by a systematic vocabulary that powers solos, intros, comping, and fills.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Below you have 26 chords which you must learn to use right away. There are more chords to learn, but these are
the most important. We have to limit ourselves to the most important chords, because, as you know, there are so
many meaningless chords for guitar that this has to be done.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the curated 26-chord vocabulary that the rest of the method numerically references, and rationalises the radical pruning by attacking 'meaningless' chord shapes.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
If you will notice, some of these chords are designed for the key of G major and some for C major. These are the
two keys that we are going to work with first. From these keys we will transpose to all of the other keys.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Anchors the method's pedagogy in G and C as the seed keys from which every other key is derived by transposition.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
It is very important that everything in the book be transposed to all of the other keys. You should be able to play
any song in any key by the time you finish this book, and learning to transpose is the only thing that can help
you to do this.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Elevates transposition from a study aid to the single non-negotiable practice that defines mastery of the method.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Get a music writing book, and write everything in it for retaining as you go on.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Prescribes the physical study artefact (hand-written transposition notebook) that the later lessons assume the student is producing.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Take your G Chords which are the first three chords in Lesson One, and practice them chromatically always up the neck of your guitar as shown below.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes Baker's signature drill: moving each chord shape chromatically up the neck to internalise voicings as portable hand-shapes.

quotes.verbatim_quote: By the way, you are only using Chords No. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 for these Exercises. Just transpose them to the proper keys.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Restricts the rhythm-chord palette to a tight numbered set, reinforcing the catalog-numbering system that underpins every subsequent comping example.

quotes.verbatim_quote: First, I'm going to write out the standard changes as they are in some songs, and below them I will write the new ones, the way they should be played against the old ones.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the two-line standard/new substitution format that becomes the dominant pedagogical device of the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Before I take you into Lesson 4, I want to remind you that every lesson in this book is important, (so you are not to skip anything!!). Give every Exercise all the study you can.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Codifies the sequential, no-skipping study discipline that the method's cumulative chord-numbering scheme depends on.

quotes.verbatim_quote: In Lesson 3 you had Chord Exercises in the key of F, G, A♭ and B♭. The six chords that we used for those four keys are the most important Rhythm Chords in these keys.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Cements the doctrine that six numbered shapes cover the four foundational keys, framing how comping vocabulary scales by transposition.
